facts.fact: Java HashMap switches a bucket from linked list to Red-Black Tree when the number of entries exceeds TREEIFY_THRESHOLD of 8 nodes, improving lookup from O(n) to O(log n)
Java HashMap uses a default load factor of 0.75, which means resize triggers at capacity * 0.75 (e.g., 16 * 0.75 = 12 for default 16-capacity map)
Quadratic probing is 95%-98% faster than linear probing with significantly reduced collisions and no increase in memory usage
Robin Hood hashing reduces probe length variance from 16.2 to 0.98 at load factor 0.9, and from 194 to 1.87 at load factor 0.99

When doubling hash table size on resize, the total cost to insert n elements is O(n) because n hashes are computed for insertions plus n + n/2 + n/4 + ... = 2n additional hashes during resizing, totaling 3n hashes
If hash table grows by fixed increment instead of doubling, insertion becomes O(n²) instead of O(n), because each element is rehashed O(n) times on average
Python 3.6+ dict uses compact layout with indices pointing to an array of entries stored in insertion order, reducing memory footprint vs pre-3.6 unordered dicts
Python dict initializes with 8-element array and ensures at least 1/3 of buckets remain empty; doubles size when space needed; uses SipHash (not standard hashing) to prevent collision DoS attacks
Separate chaining can tolerate load factors > 1.0, while open addressing must keep load factor < 0.7-0.8 to avoid clustering
Linear probing has superior cache locality but suffers from primary clustering (long runs of filled cells), while double hashing shows virtually no clustering but poor cache performance
Halkarnikar et al. (2024) introduced binary probing, a new collision resolution technique with demonstrated performance improvements over traditional methods
Cuckoo hashing guarantees O(1) worst-case lookup by maintaining two hash tables with independent functions, displacing colliding elements between tables
A good hash function must exhibit the avalanche effect: any single-bit change in input should affect ~50% of output bits
High-performance in-memory dictionaries (language runtimes) use linear probing variants for cache locality, while Redis and Memcached use chaining for dynamic key-value storage
Rustc-hash FxHashMap switched from fnv to fxhash and achieved 6% speedup in compiler benchmarks; hash function quality affects performance by 4%-84% across applications

examples.scenario: Java HashMap with default settings (capacity=16, load factor=0.75)
examples.detail: Threshold is 12 entries. On 13th insert, capacity doubles to 32 and all 13 entries are rehashed. This O(13) rehashing is amortized: average cost per insert over many operations is still O(1)

examples.scenario: Python dict handling typical function kwargs
examples.detail: CPython initializes dicts with 8 slots for common case (function arguments). For large dicts, it maintains ≥1/3 empty slots via doubling, preventing collision cascade

examples.scenario: Robin Hood hashing at production scale
examples.detail: At load factor 0.9, standard open addressing shows variance 16.2 (wide spread in probe distances), while Robin Hood variance is 0.98 (uniform probe distances). Matters for tail latency in services

After insertions, clusters form (e.g., 20 consecutive filled slots). Finding next empty slot requires probing through entire cluster. At load factor 0.9, expected probe count for linear probing approaches 50+ probes

analogies.analogy: Like a parking lot: empty (0.1 load factor) you find a spot instantly. At 75% full, still fast. At 95% full (cliff), you circle endlessly. The cliff is sharp: collision costs stay linear until threshold, then explode exponentially
Chaining = overflow parking lot outside (extra memory, no cascade). Open addressing = rearrange parked cars in existing lot (cache-friendly, clustering risk)
Like compound interest: each insert costs $3 (hash + reserve for future rehash). When resize happens, accumulated reserves pay for it. Over n inserts, cost is linear, so per-insert is O(1)

Like Robin Hood: redistribute wealth (probe distances). Rich elements (close to home slot) give up space to poor elements (far from home). Result: everyone ends up roughly equally far, reducing worst-case latency
Like shuffling a deck: one card flip should randomize the whole order. One bit flip in input should affect ~50% of output bits. Bad shuffling leaves patterns; bad hashing leaves clustering

key_numbers.metric: Default load factor (Java, Python, most implementations)
key_numbers.interpretation: Resize triggers at 75% capacity to balance time (fewer collisions) vs space (not too sparse)

key_numbers.metric: Load factor for open addressing (recommended max)
key_numbers.interpretation: Beyond 0.7, clustering/variance in probe distances degrades performance sharply for linear/quadratic probing
Chaining tolerates load factor > 1.0 because lists grow; open addressing cannot
Bucket converts from linked list to Red-Black Tree at 8 collisions to maintain O(log n) worst-case lookup in pathological cases
Tree converts back to list at 6 entries to save memory during resize
Linear probing prefetches 64-byte line efficiently; chaining jumps across memory, breaking cache

key_numbers.metric: Expected probe count (linear probing) at load 0.5
key_numbers.interpretation: Average ~1.5 probes to find/insert at 50% load; grows to 50+ at 95%

key_numbers.metric: Expected probe count (double hashing) at load 0.5
key_numbers.interpretation: Slightly better than linear probing; avoids clustering
Measured in practice; due to eliminated primary clustering

key_numbers.metric: Robin Hood probe variance at load 0.9 (open addressing)
key_numbers.interpretation: vs. 16.2 for standard open addressing; minimizes tail latency
Sufficient for most function kwargs; experiments showed 8 is practical minimum
Maintains at least 1/3 empty to prevent clustering; doubles on breach
Measured in compiler benchmarks; shows hash function quality directly impacts throughput

key_numbers.metric: Amortized rehash cost per insert (with doubling)
key_numbers.interpretation: 1 for insert + 2 amortized for future rehashes (accounting method)

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hash table with a good hash function never has collisions
common_misconceptions.truth: Collisions are unavoidable by pigeonhole principle. Even perfect hash functions collide when load > 1.0. The goal is to minimize collision *impact*, not eliminate collisions

common_misconceptions.misconception: Load factor should be kept as high as possible to save memory
common_misconceptions.truth: High load factor (>0.75) causes exponential performance degradation due to clustering. 0.75 is sweet spot: ~4% extra memory for 10-50x better performance

common_misconceptions.misconception: Linear probing is the simplest and thus best collision resolution
common_misconceptions.truth: Linear probing has worst clustering behavior. Quadratic probing or double hashing are 95-98% faster with minimal extra complexity

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hash table lookup is always O(1)
common_misconceptions.truth: With poor hash function or high load factor, lookup degrades to O(n). Java 8+ mitigates with tree fallback at 8 collisions

common_misconceptions.misconception: Growing hash table by fixed increment (e.g., +100) is fine
common_misconceptions.truth: Fixed increment causes O(n²) amortized insertion time (each element rehashed O(n) times). Exponential growth (doubling) maintains O(n) total cost

common_misconceptions.misconception: Chaining always uses less memory than open addressing
common_misconceptions.truth: Chaining adds pointer overhead per node (8+ bytes). Modern implementations (Java 8+, Python 3.6+) use compact layouts that can be more efficient than poorly-implemented chaining

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hash function quality doesn't matter if collision resolution is good
common_misconceptions.truth: Poor hash function causes clustering regardless of resolution method. Avalanche effect (bit-flipping sensitivity) is mandatory; rushing hashing leaves distribution artifacts

common_misconceptions.misconception: Rehashing during resize is wasted work
common_misconceptions.truth: Rehashing cost is amortized O(1) per insert when done with doubling. The O(n) rehash cost spreads across n subsequent inserts, each 'paying' for it incrementally

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cuckoo hashing is always faster than chaining
common_misconceptions.truth: Cuckoo hashing guarantees O(1) worst-case but has high constant factors and insertion complexity. Best for read-heavy workloads; chaining better for write-heavy

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cache performance is irrelevant for hash tables
common_misconceptions.truth: Open addressing's linear memory layout is 2-3x faster than chaining's pointer-chased list (L3 cache miss vs. L1 hit). Matters in production; negligible in academic tests
